name: Wind Bell
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly references early 20th century European advertising, Art Nouveau aesthetics and 1960s psychedelia, placing Wind Bell firmly in the ornamental display tradition with wavy, expressive letterforms.
summary: An Art Nouveau-influenced display face drawing on early 20th century European advertising and 1960s psychedelic typography.
description: Wind Bell channels the warbly, organic lettering of Belle Époque advertising posters and the hallucinatory type experiments of 1960s counterculture print. The result is a display face suited to projects that want period atmosphere without resorting to straight pastiche. Works well for branding, editorial headers and packaging where a dreamlike, handcrafted character is called for.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi Type describe Wind Bell as inspired by early 20th century European advertising, evoking absinthe-soaked Art Nouveau pages and 1960s psychedelic experiments. They position it as a display face with a warbly, dream-inducing quality suited to any project needing a distinctive twist of soul.

name: Herba Buena
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a classic script, suited to book covers, food and beverage branding, and wordmarks, placing it firmly in the script category with a traditional, pen-influenced style.
summary: A classic script designed for food, beverage and editorial branding, with a broad application range from book covers to farm-fresh packaging.
description: Herba Buena is a classic script from Wabi Sabi Type, positioned for packaging, wordmarks and editorial work where a calligraphic hand is called for. The foundry highlights its versatility across elevated dining, tonic brands and book covers. Its character sits in the well-worn tradition of pen-based scripts suited to organic and artisan aesthetics.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi Type describe Herba Buena as a personal studio favourite, a classic script that springs vigorously from a long-dormant seed. They position it for book covers, sparkling tonics, farm-fresh products, elevated dining and a wide variety of wordmarks.

name: Magnolia
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a script, with aesthetic references to midcentury radio cards, ice cream trucks and Victorian woven labels, placing it firmly in the decorative retro script category.
summary: A retro-leaning script drawing on midcentury Americana and Victorian label aesthetics, designed to work as both a headline and a supporting element.
description: Built with enough character to anchor a logotype yet restrained enough to work in supporting roles, Magnolia references midcentury radio cards, ice cream trucks and Victorian woven labels. The foundry highlights its tweakability for bespoke logotype work, suggesting it suits branding projects where a period flavour is needed without heavy customisation. Its broad tonal range across historical references gives it versatility across packaging, signage and identity work.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi Type describe Magnolia as their second script in the Wabi Sabi archive — strong enough to steal the show but utilitarian enough to play a supporting role. They note it is easily tweakable for bespoke logotypes and evokes midcentury radio cards, ice cream trucks, belt buckle champions and Victorian-era woven labels, adaptable to any creative world a designer might be building.

name: Black Copal
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly describes it as a blackletter, tracing its lineage from Western European black-letter traditions through to the Americas. The name and descriptive copy confirm a calligraphic blackletter form.
summary: A calligraphic blackletter rooted in European manuscript tradition and filtered through a Central and North American cultural lens.
description: Black Copal draws on the legacy of amateur calligraphy, tracing a lineage from the blackletter scripts of Western Europe through to the American continent. The foundry frames it as a modest, memory-hewn form, passed between cultures rather than institutionally refined. Suited to display work where historical gravity and handmade warmth are both required.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi Type describe Black Copal as inspired by past generations of amateur calligraphers, a blackletter jogged from memory that travels from the black forests and gilded pages of Western Europe to the dusty hills and expanses of Central and North America, handed down from one culture to another.

name: Modern Ephemera
classification_notes: Described as a handpainted spirit drawn from archival sources, placing it firmly in the display category as an expressive, hand-rendered typeface with no indication of a structural serif or sans classification.
summary: A hand-rendered display typeface drawn from archival, hand-painted lettering traditions with a deliberately imperfect, human quality.
description: Rooted in the hand-painted lettering of the past, this typeface carries the irregular strokes and organic warmth of found ephemera. Wabi Sabi describe it as a founding piece for the studio, suggesting it set the tone for their broader aesthetic direction. Best suited to branding, packaging or editorial work that calls for an authentic, imperfect hand.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi describe Modern Ephemera as one of their most beloved tools and an original spirit that started the foundry's journey. They sourced it by digging through archival, hand-painted lettering, presenting it as a reminder of shared humanity and the value of individual difference.

name: Spiritist
classification_notes: The evocative copy referencing beat poets, card catalogues and makers marks suggests a characterful display face with vintage or antique qualities, likely a display serif with artisanal character.
summary: A vintage-inflected display face from the studio of Land, evoking beat poetry, dusty card catalogues and the understated marks of craftspeople.
description: Described by the foundry as one of Land's most-used tools, Spiritist carries the quiet character of ephemera and hand-stamped relics rather than loud decorative excess. Its appeal lies in utility tempered by personality, making it suited to branding, editorial work and packaging where a worn or archival sensibility is wanted. The foundry's copy positions it as a trusted workhorse rather than a novelty.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi Type describe Spiritist as an oldie but a goodie from the studio of Land, citing its utility and true beauty. They evoke forgotten dreams, beat poets, dusty card catalogues and understated makers marks on priceless relics, calling it one of Land's most used tools in the old type shed.

name: Juniper
classification_notes: Described as having a subtle spur rather than a full serif, placing it in the spur or semi-serif category, sitting between a sans and a traditional serif in character.
summary: A utilitarian spur-serif with restrained detailing that sits comfortably between conventional serif and sans-serif territory.
description: Juniper uses a minimal spur rather than a full serif, giving it an understated quality suited to a broad range of applications. The foundry positions it as a balance between refined and workaday, making it adaptable for both display and general-purpose use. Its restrained character keeps it from feeling precious while retaining enough typographic personality to stand apart from neutral grotesques.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi Type describe Juniper as an engraver's dream, with the softest and most subtle spur lending this utilitarian font wide appeal. They position it as striking a balance between high and low, calling it a true beauty suited to a wide range of projects and scenarios.

name: Choya
classification_notes: Described as coming straight from the brush of a letter maker with calligraphic energy, placing it firmly in the brush script category with a casual, hand-lettered quality.
summary: A brush script with calligraphic looseness and a sun-baked Southwestern character, somewhere between a skilled signpainter and a relaxed afternoon.
description: Choya draws on the calligraphic tradition of hand-lettered display work, with the kind of swirling energy associated with pre-digital Latin American and American Southwest vernacular graphics. The foundry describes it as coming from a knowledgeable letter maker but with a whimsy that suggests heat, leisure and mild intoxication. Well suited to packaging, labels and editorial work that wants warmth and period character without tipping into parody.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi Type describe Choya as a swirl of nostalgic calligraphic energy conjuring the spirit of everything south of New Mexico and from an era before the present day. Produced directly from the brush of a skilled letter maker, it carries a slight whimsy suggesting relaxing herbs, a few potent pints or too long in the desert, and is described as perfect for many instances.

name: Long View
classification_notes: The references to stamped metal badges, hand-drawn elevations and typewritten pages suggest a restrained, utilitarian display face with serif or letterpress-influenced characteristics suited to headline and decorative use.
summary: A restrained display face drawing on stamped metal badges, hand-drawn architectural elevations and the texture of typewritten pages.
description: Long View trades ornament for clarity, channelling the quiet authority of mid-century utility print and mechanical letterforms. The foundry frames it around simplicity and the formal possibilities of words on a page, evoking letterpress output and badge printing rather than calligraphic tradition. Suited to editorial headlines, packaging and branding where a worn, unaffected quality is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi Type describe Long View as a celebration of simplicity, restraining decorative tendencies to let the message take precedence. They evoke stamped metal badges, hand-drawn elevations and poetry typed on machines that now gather dust as the spirit behind the design.

name: Corsicana
classification_notes: Corsicana is a mono-weight blocky display typeface drawing on Americana signpainting and Latin American modernism, placing it firmly in the display category with a hand-lettered, vernacular sans character.
summary: A mono-weight display face drawing on American signpainting traditions and Latin American modernism, with a blocky, hand-touched character.
description: Corsicana pulls from two distinct vernacular traditions: the mono-weight block lettering of old Main Street signage and a Latin American modernist sensibility. The result is a display typeface that carries the warmth of the human hand without leaning too heavily on nostalgia. It suits brand identities, packaging and editorial work where a sense of place and craft matters.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi Type describe Corsicana as a typeface that covers a lot of ground, drifting between the mono-weight blocky type of old Main Street brickwork and a contemporary hunger for the human hand. Inspired simultaneously by Americana signpainter utopias and Latin American modernism, they position it as a typeface that can take you anywhere you long to be.

name: Existence
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly describes Existence as blending calligraphy and script type, with elegant forms that evoke soft waves; this places it firmly in the calligraphic script subcategory.
summary: A calligraphic script typeface from Wabi Sabi Type with fluid, wave-like letterforms suited to refined, handcrafted branding projects.
description: Drawing from both calligraphy and script type traditions, Existence produces letterforms described as mimicking soft waves of grasses and smoke. The design leans into unhurried, organic elegance rather than formal penmanship, making it adaptable across packaging, editorial headers and identity work where a gentle, handmade quality is wanted. Priced at $99 as a single purchase from Wabi Sabi Type's broader collection of artisanal display faces.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi Type describe Existence as channelling a slower moment in time, blending calligraphy and script type so the user can move between both disciplines. They note its elegant forms mimic the soft waves of grasses and smoke, positioning it as ideal for projects that call for quality, timelessness and a gentle hand.

name: Yarrow
classification_notes: The descriptive copy evokes rugged, sun-baked Americana and high desert imagery, suggesting a characterful display typeface with regional personality rather than a neutral text face.
summary: A display typeface drawn from the visual culture of the American high desert, with a roaming, hand-crafted character suited to editorial and branding work.
description: Yarrow takes its cues from the dusty landscapes of West Texas and the Sonoran Desert, positioning itself as a typeface with regional grit and an unhurried, organic quality. The foundry describes it as roaming hills, valleys and open roads, suggesting letterforms with personality rather than neutrality. It fits naturally into packaging, editorial and branding projects that want warmth and a sense of place.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi Type describe Yarrow as the result of mixing West Texas dust, dried Mexican herbs and Sonoran Desert rainwater, a font that roams the hills, valleys and open roads of the high desert.

name: Flora
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a block font with soft, heavy forms; the tactile, dimensional character places it firmly in display territory with no indication of text-weight usage.
summary: A heavy block display typeface with softened edges, designed to evoke physicality and tactile craft processes.
description: Flora is a block font built around soft, weighty letterforms that suggest materials like plaster, clay and pressed paper. The rounded heaviness gives it a minimal boldness suited to short-form display use, headlines and packaging. Its character sits somewhere between rough craft and clean geometry, evoking physical making rather than digital precision.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi Type describe Flora as a playful block font that yearns for a tactile existence, with a soft but heavy presence suited to three-dimensional applications such as sculpted plaster walls, pressed earth blocks and debossed paper for bold, minimal expression.

name: Yantra
classification_notes: Yantra draws from Indian amateur signpainting vernacular and classic wood type traditions, placing it firmly in the display category with a vernacular, exotica-inflected character.
summary: A display typeface inspired by Indian amateur signpainting traditions and classic wood type, carrying an esoteric and exotica-inflected character.
description: Yantra takes its cues from India's vernacular signpainting culture, channelling the hand-made energy and idiosyncratic letterforms found in amateur painted lettering. The foundry positions it as an outlier within the wood type tradition, something that sits apart from the familiar canon. It suits work that calls for an eccentric, culturally evocative display presence.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi Type describe Yantra as an esoteric black sheep to classic wood types, inspired by the vast world of India's amateur signpainting vernacular. They say it conjures the spirits of exotica and reminds us there is a whole universe beyond our small, familiar worlds.

name: Warbler
classification_notes: The foundry describes Warbler as simultaneously modern and classic, bold and gentle, suggesting a humanist sans that balances warmth with utility rather than a strict geometric or grotesque approach.
summary: A versatile, utility-minded sans from Wabi Sabi Type pitched as an all-purpose supporting face for brand work.
description: Warbler is positioned as a workhorse typeface, adaptable enough to suit a wide range of brand aesthetics without demanding attention. The foundry frames it as sitting somewhere between loud and quiet, modern and classic, suggesting a humanist sensibility that holds up in both display and text contexts. Priced at $99, it is listed as a studio favourite at Wabi Sabi Type.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi Type describe Warbler as a universal and utilitarian typeface suited to just about any occasion, calling it a perfect supporting font for any brand aesthetic. They note it plays many parts — simultaneously loud and quiet, modern and classic, bold and gentle — and refer to it as a studio favourite they are glad to release.

name: Curio
classification_notes: The foundry describes it as rooted in 19th-century craft traditions associated with hyper-realistic decorative painting, placing it firmly in the ornamental Victorian display category.
summary: A Victorian-era decorative display face revived from 19th-century craft traditions, originally associated with artisans who painted hyper-realistic marble and wood textures.
description: Rooted in the decorative trades of the 1800s, Curio draws on letterforms produced by craftspeople whose work spanned signage and ornamental surface painting. The style carries the period's characteristic richness and universality, lending itself to boutique branding, editorial headings and packaging that want a sense of antique refinement. The foundry positions it as a timeless design capable of sitting naturally across a wide range of historical and contemporary contexts.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi Type describe Curio as dusted off from the lost vaults of time, a beloved type originally created by craftspeople who also painted hyper-realistic marble and wooden textures in the 1800s. They position it as a truly universal style that could grace the halls and boutiques of any time and place.

name: Brother Wind
classification_notes: The foundry's own copy references calligraphic flow and letterforms evoking natural, meditative imagery, placing this firmly in the calligraphic script category.
summary: A calligraphic script face inspired by Zen aesthetics and natural movement, built around flowing, unhurried letterforms.
description: Brother Wind draws on calligraphic traditions to create letterforms with a slow, meditative quality, evoking incense smoke and drifting seeds rather than urgency or precision. The design sits in a space between formal calligraphy and organic hand lettering, with forms described as reclined yet upright. Suited to branding and editorial contexts where a contemplative, nature-adjacent tone is required.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi Type describe Brother Wind as a calligraphic typeface whose letterforms evoke serenity and natural movement, from swaying branches to weightless seeds drifting in the breeze. They liken its rhythm to a mountain sitting in zazen, unhurried and present, with a calligraphic flow that listens for answers on the shores of an endless river.

name: Peace Flag
classification_notes: Described as a bold monotype with playful, dreamy character and fluid hand-touched qualities; the stackable and archable features place it firmly in the display/novelty category.
summary: A bold, playful display typeface with a hand-touched quality, designed to be stacked and arched for expressive typographic arrangements.
description: Peace Flag leans into deliberate imperfection, softening what is otherwise a dense and intense monotype construction with a loose, human warmth. Its stackable and archable capabilities make it well-suited to logotype work, posters and packaging where a freewheeling, handmade spirit is wanted. The foundry positions it as a typeface that takes lightness seriously.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi Type describe Peace Flag as a dreamy and playful take on an otherwise bold and intense monotype, designed to bring fluidity and a human touch to a project. They highlight its stackable and archable qualities and frame the typeface around a celebration of imperfection and lightness of spirit.

name: Forest Retreat
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly describes it as a marriage of blackletter and slab, drawing on early monastic manuscripts and folk art traditions, placing it firmly in the blackletter category with a distinctive slab hybrid quality.
summary: A blackletter and slab hybrid drawn from early monastic manuscripts and folk art traditions, rendered with a hard-edged, chiselled quality.
description: Conceived as a meeting of blackletter and slab serif, Forest Retreat takes its cues from illuminated manuscripts and folk art found in Black Forest temples. The letterforms suggest marks made with a hard-edged quill pressed into soft paper or cut into old timber, giving the face a crude, tactile character. Best suited to display work where an archaic, handcrafted atmosphere is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi Type describe Forest Retreat as an exploration of early monastic manuscripts and illuminated folk art from Black Forest temples, imagining letterforms etched with a hard-edged quill or chiselled into old-growth wood. They position it as a playful merging of the ancient and modern through a crude marriage of blackletter and slab.

name: Futurista
classification_notes: Inspired by Fortunato Depero's Italian Futurist hand-cut lettering, the typeface is a display face with a playful, expressive character suited to graphic and commercial work rather than running text.
summary: A display typeface drawn from the hand-cut lettering of Italian Futurist artist Fortunato Depero, balancing playful character with practical legibility.
description: Rooted in the visual language of Fortunato Depero's early twentieth-century Futurist work, the typeface channels his hand-cut letterforms and bold graphic sensibility into a usable display font. Wabi Sabi Type describe a deliberate tension between playfulness and legibility, treating the two as compatible rather than opposing. Best suited to branding, editorial headlines and packaging where a vintage, expressive energy is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi Type describe Futurista as heavily inspired by Italian Futurist artist Fortunato Depero, whose hand-cut lettering and experimental layouts demonstrated that art and commerce could coexist. They cite his playful letterforms as a formative influence, and position the typeface as offering both that childlike spirit and genuine legibility.

name: Universal
classification_notes: The foundry's own description references breeze blocks, temple glyphs and a 'soft brutalist and robust deco spirit', pointing to a decorative geometric display face with architectural and art deco influences rather than a text typeface.
summary: A display face drawing on breeze-block geometry and ancient temple glyphs, landing somewhere between soft brutalism and art deco.
description: Designed around the forms of modernist breeze blocks and historical temple carvings, the typeface blends architectural geometry with a decorative deco sensibility. The foundry frames it as culturally ambiguous, giving it a range of contexts from monumental lettering to contemporary graphic work. Its character sits closest to typefaces intended to be stamped, chiselled or cast rather than set at small sizes.
foundry_blurb: Wabi Sabi Type describe Universal as inspired by modern breeze blocks and ancient temple glyphs, presenting it as an architectural typeface that balances form and function. They characterise it as having a soft brutalist and art deco spirit, fit to be cast in bronze, chiselled into limestone or stamped into terra cotta, with a cultural ambiguity that opens it to a wide range of applications.
